nativeRange.habitat: The Miniatus Grouper is a reef-associated species that tends to be found in the clear waters of coral reefs, more often along exposed outer reef faces than in sheltered lagoons. It has been recorded at depths ranging from 2 to 150 m.

diet.notes: Naturally feeds on small fishes (reportedly up to 80% of diet) and crustaceans. In captivity has been documented accepting freeze-dried krill soaked in Selcon, frozen preparations, squid, and silversides. Some specimens may initially require live feeders for conditioning before accepting prepared foods.

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 2930x2198. Tack-sharp full lateral view of a vivid orange-red adult covered in electric-blue spots against a clean dark background - textbook diagnostic coloration.
Mohammed Al Momany / NOAA; 1820x1192. Whole crimson-red individual with white-blue spots over reef; sharp, well-lit, public domain. Different (deeper red) color angle for variety.
Diego Delso; 6059x4039. Crisp head-and-shoulders portrait showing the vivid blue spots and blue-ringed eye in fine detail; tail cropped so kept as a non-recommended feature close-up.

summary: A brilliantly colored Indo-Pacific grouper with a vivid orange-red body covered in electric-blue spots. Best kept by intermediate hobbyists with very large systems; tends to be aggressive toward smaller tankmates.
careNotes: The Miniatus Grouper, also commonly sold under the names Coral Hind and Coral Grouper, is considered one of the more visually striking groupers available in the marine trade, with an orange-red body covered in small vivid blue spots. Wild specimens have been documented reaching 50 cm, while captive fish generally grow to around 35–40 cm. A tank of at least 700 liters is generally advised to accommodate an adult comfortably. Only one specimen per tank is typically recommended, as conspecifics and other grouper species have been known to fight. This species is generally regarded as reef-safe with caution — it will readily consume small fish and ornamental shrimp but tends to leave corals undisturbed. Newly acquired specimens sometimes require live feeders initially but have been reported to transition to frozen and prepared foods once acclimated. Stable reef water conditions are recommended: a specific gravity of 1.020–1.025, pH of 8.1–8.4, and a carbonate hardness (kH) of 8–12. In the wild, this species is a protogynous hermaphrodite — meaning all individuals begin life as females, with the dominant individual in a social group eventually transitioning to male. Wild groups typically consist of a single male holding a territory shared by 2–12 females.
breedingNotes: The Miniatus Grouper is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning all individuals begin life as female and the dominant fish in a social group will eventually transition to male. In the wild, males maintain haremic groups of 2–12 females, each holding territories that can span hundreds of square meters. Captive spawning has not been reliably documented in home aquaria.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (50 cm TL wild max vs 14 in/35.6 cm captive per LiveAquaria), temperature range (23.7–29°C vs 72–78°F/22.2–25.6°C — averaged to 23–27°C), native range, taxonomy, diet composition, and haremic social structure. FishBase also confirmed protogynous hermaphroditism and maximum reported age ~30 years.

fish.breedingNotes: The Miniatus Grouper is a protogynous hermaphrodite, forming haremic groups in the wild. Captive spawning has not been reliably documented.
